caveats: a: The occupational mix is a three-digit beverage-and-tobacco proxy.
a: Estate vineyard labor is difficult to separate from winery production labor.
a: GenAI task exposure is not equivalent to current unautomated substitutable labor.
rho: No representative U.S. winery study of realized AI labor savings was found.
rho: Small firms may lack integrated customer, depletion, inventory, and production data.
rho: The estimate excludes autonomous field labor and speculative general-purpose robotics.
e: Industry databases count facilities and virtual wineries differently from Census firms.
e: Production volume does not map cleanly to EBITDA because bottle price and DTC mix vary widely.
e: The frozen count uses a food-processing margin bridge that may misclassify hospitality and agricultural economics.
s5: The owner-age statistic is cross-industry and based on 2018 respondents.
s5: Reported transaction counts emphasize notable deals and omit confidential small transactions.
s5: Wine deals often separate brands, inventory, vineyards, and production facilities, only some of which qualify.
q: The public-company channel mix is one Oregon winery and is not representative nationally.
q: Channel mix, bottle price, club penetration, and distributor power vary greatly by region and brand.
q: Discounting may absorb savings even without explicit contractual pass-through.
d5: Consumption estimates cover imported wine and producers outside the lens.
d5: Pandemic years amplify the apparent recent decline.
d5: Premium, direct, private-label, and custom-crush cohorts can diverge sharply from total gallons.
o: Operator requirement does not imply survival of each winery or brand.
o: Custom crush preserves accountable production while shifting it to another operator.
o: Imports and large domestic facilities can replace LMM production without software eliminating the service.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring case, bottle, bulk, private-label, and custom-production sales to distributors, retailers, restaurants, club members, and brand owners, with wholesale and direct-to-consumer channels often coexisting.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS-linked winery populations mix physical production, estate agriculture, virtual brands, tasting rooms, clubs, tourism, and events. To keep labor, retention, and operator-requirement judgments coherent, the lens retains accountable production wineries with repeat supply or club relationships and excludes brand-only and hospitality-dominant businesses.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospitality- or events-dominant tasting-room properties; stand-alone vineyards without winery operations; brand-only virtual wineries that outsource all physical production; merchant wholesalers that only bottle purchased wine; captive pilot sites; shells; and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. winery operators that repeatedly produce, age, blend, bottle, or supply wine for external wholesale, retail, wine-club, private-label, or custom-crush customers, including estate wineries with a material production business.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 312000 beverage-and-tobacco workforce rather than wineries: production is 22.11%, transportation/material moving 12.33%, office support 4.87%, and sales 12.15% of employment. ILO task research finds clerical work most exposed to GenAI. I map high exposure in club and order administration, customer service, distributor reporting, compliance drafting, bookkeeping, purchasing, planning, marketing, and sales analysis, plus narrower forecasting and vision uses, onto production-winery work. Estate vineyard labor, cellar work, sensory decisions, lab work, sanitation, barrel handling, bottling, warehousing, and maintenance remain physical; the broad subsector proxy can therefore err in either direction.
caveats: The occupational mix is a three-digit beverage-and-tobacco proxy.
Estate vineyard labor is difficult to separate from winery production labor.
GenAI task exposure is not equivalent to current unautomated substitutable labor.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit payroll wages by detailed occupation, combined with task diaries across harvest and non-harvest periods from cellar, lab, bottling, warehouse, vineyard, club, sales, finance, compliance, and planning staff at at least fifteen LMM wineries, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Production wineries carry a sizable digital coordination and commercial layer around long-cycle physical inventory. AI can substitute portions of order, club, sales, finance, compliance, inventory, and planning work and improve inspection, but cannot replace most vineyard, crush, cellar, laboratory, bottling, or warehouse tasks. The base reflects partial task opportunity rather than whole-job automation.

d5.bridge: Wine Institute estimates total U.S. wine consumption fell from 1.06 billion gallons in 2021 to 870 million in 2024 and per-resident consumption from 3.16 to 2.54 gallons; its California shipment series also shows U.S.-market cases below 2019 levels. These are broad wine quantities, not a five-year forecast for the narrowed LMM production-winery basket. I project continued but moderated decline rather than mechanically repeating the pandemic-normalization drop, allowing differentiated producers and private-label or custom-crush work to outperform. Broad consumption may overstate decline for premium survivors or understate it where distributor rationalization hits small wineries hardest.
caveats: Consumption estimates cover imported wine and producers outside the lens.
Pandemic years amplify the apparent recent decline.
Premium, direct, private-label, and custom-crush cohorts can diverge sharply from total gallons.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of NIQ or Circana equivalent-volume sales by price tier and channel, Sovos ShipCompliant direct-shipment cases, distributor depletion data, TTB removals, and custom-crush volumes matched to LMM winery cohorts would support a constant-quality forecast.
d5.rationale: The current wine service basket faces a sustained quantity contraction driven by lower per-capita consumption, changing alcohol preferences, distributor and retail consolidation, and inventory overhang. Premiumization can support dollars but does not preserve constant-quality quantity. The high case allows stabilization; the low case reflects continued mid-single-digit annual erosion.

e.bridge: WineBusiness Analytics counts 11,165 unique virtual and bonded U.S. wineries, with 82% producing fewer than 5,000 cases and only 18% at 5,000 cases or more; WineAmerica explicitly includes own-brand producers, contract production facilities, virtual marketers, estate operations, tasting rooms, and warehouses. These are facility and industry-population descriptions, not the 696 margin-bridged LMM firms. I infer that LMM scale selects toward operating producers, while subtracting brand-only virtual, hospitality-dominant, and nontransferable estate structures. The bridge may overstate eligibility if food-processing margins classify many tourism-heavy wineries into the band.
caveats: Industry databases count facilities and virtual wineries differently from Census firms.
Production volume does not map cleanly to EBITDA because bottle price and DTC mix vary widely.
The frozen count uses a food-processing margin bridge that may misclassify hospitality and agricultural economics.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level roster of the 696 estimated candidates with EBITDA, production cases, bonded premises, custom-crush activity, virtual status, channel revenue, hospitality revenue, estate ownership, and transferable operating assets would directly classify eligibility.
e.rationale: Most wineries sell repeatedly to consumers, distributors, retailers, restaurants, or brand owners, and production or custom-crush operators fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by virtual brands with no accountable plant, tasting-room or events businesses whose labor economics are hospitality-led, and estates where wine production is secondary or not separately transferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 312130 ($4,244,843,908) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 312130 ($24,952,086,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $382,748 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 312130); class <5: 1,801 firms, avg revenue/firm $375,421 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $57,252 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 969 firms, avg revenue/firm $981,712 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $149,711 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 800 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,859,065 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $283,507 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 632 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,663,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,168,712 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $61,281,031 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,345,357 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $653,599,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $99,673,878 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 696

caveats: Operator requirement does not imply survival of each winery or brand.
Custom crush preserves accountable production while shifting it to another operator.
Imports and large domestic facilities can replace LMM production without software eliminating the service.
o.change_evidence: TTB removals and production by premises size, custom-crush throughput, virtual-brand sourcing, import shares by price tier, winery permit exits, and production transfers after acquisitions would measure migration away from LMM production operators.
o.rationale: Wine is a physical, regulated, vintage-sensitive product requiring an accountable operator to crush, ferment, age, blend, test, record, bottle, and release it. Software supports those tasks but cannot supply the product. Some demand may migrate from LMM wineries to imports, large producers, virtual brands using scaled custom-crush facilities, or adjacent beverages, so the share remaining with an operator of the narrowed kind is below one.

caveats: The public-company channel mix is one Oregon winery and is not representative nationally.
Channel mix, bottle price, club penetration, and distributor power vary greatly by region and brand.
Discounting may absorb savings even without explicit contractual pass-through.
q.change_evidence: Channel-level net-price waterfalls, club terms and churn, distributor contracts, depletion allowances, promotion accruals, direct-shipping costs, custom-crush contracts, and gross-margin changes around documented productivity deployments would directly measure five-year sharing.
q.rationale: Wine clubs and direct sales can retain efficiency gains between price changes, while wholesale channels split economics across distributors, retailers, and restaurants. One public LMM-sized winery reported 54.4% direct sales and 45.6% distributor sales in 2025, with gross margin nearly flat but higher discounts contributing to pressure. A shrinking category increases promotions and bargaining, so durable retention should be material but incomplete.

caveats: No representative U.S. winery study of realized AI labor savings was found.
Small firms may lack integrated customer, depletion, inventory, and production data.
The estimate excludes autonomous field labor and speculative general-purpose robotics.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post labor hours, headcount, club churn, forecast error, inventory write-downs, compliance exceptions, order-service levels, and production yield from comparable winery AI deployments would narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Within five years, wineries can implement AI-assisted demand and depletion forecasting, inventory allocation, club retention outreach, order service, compliance preparation, purchasing, production scheduling, marketing workflows, and quality anomaly detection. Realized labor avoidance is constrained by fragmented winery software, distributor data gaps, vintage-specific judgment, regulated records and labels, seasonal peaks, physical workflows, and the need for accountable sensory and release decisions.

caveats: The owner-age statistic is cross-industry and based on 2018 respondents.
Reported transaction counts emphasize notable deals and omit confidential small transactions.
Wine deals often separate brands, inventory, vineyards, and production facilities, only some of which qualify.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of TTB permit ownership changes, deeds, UCC records, broker closings, SBA and bank acquisition loans, and brand-plus-operating-asset transactions matched to the eligible LMM winery roster would measure qualifying control transfers and distinguish them from closures and partial asset sales.
s5.rationale: Broad succession pressure is meaningful because 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older in 2018. Current wine weakness is producing asset sales and consolidation, but a 2025 market report counted only 27 notable transactions through October, and recent SEC filings show that acquisitions may involve brands, inventory, customer lists, vineyards, or entire operations. The base therefore allows elevated transfer activity without treating closures or brand trades as qualifying firm transfers.

principal_driver: Long-cycle inventory and mixed wholesale, club, and custom-production operations create valuable AI-addressable planning and commercial workflows.
principal_weakness: Falling wine consumption and fragmented winery business models can overwhelm labor-efficiency gains.
